summary:
Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated on April 4, 1968, while standing on the balcony of the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, Tennessee, where he had come to support striking sanitation workers. He was 39 years old. King was shot by James Earl Ray, a white escaped convict who had been tracking him for months. Ray fled to Canada and eventually to England, where he was captured two months later.

King's assassination triggered an immediate wave of riots in over 100 American cities, resulting in dozens of deaths, thousands of injuries, and widespread property destruction. The shock and grief of the Black community and many white Americans reflected King's status as the preeminent moral voice of the [[event:2679|Civil Rights Movement]] and his global stature as a Nobel Peace Prize laureate. President Johnson called for calm and declared a national day of mourning.

King's death came at a moment when the civil rights coalition was under strain, as King had expanded his focus to economic inequality, poverty, and opposition to the Vietnam War — positions that had cost him political allies in Washington. His assassination removed from the movement its most gifted communicator and the practitioner of nonviolent strategy who had guided its greatest legislative victories. The subsequent decade saw the diffusion of the civil rights movement into multiple strands and the rise of Black Power and Black nationalist politics as alternatives to King's integrationist approach.
